coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensing of tumor suppressor protein p53 in unprocessed human plasma and cell lysates using a novel nanocomposite based on poly-cysteine/graphene quantum dots/gold nanoparticle
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Baghban, Hossein Navay
Shadjou, Nasrin
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensor for quantitation of tumor suppressor protein p53 based on ternary signal amplification strategy was fabricated. In this work, p53-antibody was immobilized onto a green and biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly l-cysteine (P-Cys) as conductive matrix and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/gold nanoparticles (GNPs) as dual amplification elements. Therefore, a novel multilayer film based on P-Cys, GQDs, and GNPs was exploited to develop a highly sensitive immunosensor for detection of p53. Fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a gold surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high amount of the anti-p53. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for p53 concentration was linear up to 0.000197-0.016 pM (by SWV technique) and 0.195–50 pM (by DPV technique) with lower limit of quantification of 0.065 fM. Also, linear range and lower limit of quantification of p53 in unprocessed human plasma were 0.000592-1.296 pM and 0.065 fM, respectively. The method was applied to the assay of p53 in human plasma sample and normal and malignant cell line lysates such as normal cell Line from mouse C3H (L929), colon cancer cell-HCT, prostate cancer cell line PC-3, and human breast adenocarcinoma cell line-MCF7.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical immunosensor
P53 tumor biomarker
Gold nanoparticles
Poly cysteine
Graphene quantum dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electropolymerization of l-cys on the surface of Au
Electrodeposition of GQDs on the surface of P-Cys modified Au electrode
Sonoelectrodeposition of GNPs on the surface of P-Cys modified Au electrode
The SEM and EDX characterization of the modified sensing interface

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The possible polymerization of l-cys on the surface of Au electrode.
The schematic illustration of the label free electrochemical immunosensor for detection of tumor suppressor protein p53 with ternary signal amplification using P-Cys, GQDs, and GNPs.

A) Electropolymerization of l-cys on the surface of Au electrode. The cyclic voltammogram depicts gradual growth of P-Cys after 10 successive cycles in the range of −1 to +1V vs. Ag/AgCl with scan rate of 0.1V/s. B) CVs for electrodeposition of GQDs on P-Cys-Au electrode using 30 cycles in the potential range of 0–1V vs. Ag/AgCl with 0.1V/s scan rate. Inset; CVs for electrodeposition of GQDs on P-Cys-Au electrode using 30 cycles in the potential range of 0–1V vs.Ag/AgCl with 0.1V/s scan rate. C) Chronoamperogram for the electrochemical deposition of GNPs on P-Cys-GQDs/Au electrode. The applied potential E=−1.8V vs. Ag/AgCl and the record time was 500s.
SEM images of the sequential modified layers on Au electrode: A) GNPs, B) P-Cys, C) P-Cys/GNPs, D) P-Cys/GQDs, E) P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs, F) P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/Streptavidin-HRP in different magnification.
EDX spectra of A) P-Cys, B) P-Cys/GNPs, C) P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs, D) P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/Streptavidin-HRP.

A) SWVs of Au, Au/P-Cys, Au/GQDs/P-Cys, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA, and Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA/p53 in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4). Step potential=0.005, Amplitude time=0.02s, Frequency=10Hz, Scan Rate=0.05V/s. B) DPVs of Au, Au/P-Cys, Au/GQDs/P-Cys, AU/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab, Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA, and Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA/p53 in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4). Step potential=0.005V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation amplitude=0.025V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation time=0.05s, Interval time=0.1s, Scan Rate=0.01V/s.

A) SWVs of Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA/p53 in 0.1M PBS (a), 0.1M PBS+0.067M thionine (b), and 0.1M PBS+0.03M thionine+1mM H2O2 (c). Step potential=0.005, Amplitude time=0.02s, Frequency=10Hz, Scan Rate=0.05V/s. B) DPVs of Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA/p53 in 0.1M PBS (a), 0.1MPBS+0.01mM thionine (b), and 0.1MPBS+ 0.067mM thionine+1mM H2O2 (c). Step potential=0.005V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation amplitude=0.025V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation time=0.05s, Interval time=0.1s, Scan Rate=0.01V/s.

A) DPVs of Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA in 0.1M PBS+0.03M thionine+1mM H2O2 in different concentrations of p53 (0, 0.0000219, 0.0000658, 0.000197, 0.00059, 0.00533, 0.016, 0.144, 0.432, 1.296,and 11.66 pM). Step potential=0.005V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation amplitude=0.025V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation time=0.05s, Interval time=0.1s, Scan Rate=0.01V/s. Inset; Calibration curve for detection of p53 (SD=4.02, n=5). B) SWVs of Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA in 0.1M PBS+0.03M thionine+1mM H2O2 in different concentrations of p53 (0, 0.0000219, 0.0000658, 0.000197, 0.00059, 0.00533, 0.016, 0.144, 0.432, 1.296,and 11.66 pM 0). Step potential=0.001, Amplitude time=0.02s, Frequency=10Hz, Scan Rate=0.05V/s. Inset; Calibration curve for detection of p53 (SD=3.21, n=5).

A) DPVs of Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA in unprocessed human plasma samples after incubation with p53 protein in 0.1MPBS+0.03M thionine+1mM H2O2 in different concentrations (0, 0.0000219, 0.0000658, 0.000197, 0.00059, 0.144, 0.432, 1.296,and 11.66 pM). Step potential=0.005V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation amplitude=0.025V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation time=0.05s, Interval time=0.1s, Scan Rate=0.01V/s. Inset; Calibration curve for detection of p53 antibodies (SD=3.25, n=5).

A) SWVs of Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA/p53 in 0.1M PBS+0.03M thionine+1mM H2O2 in the presence of normal lysates (L929), and cancer cell lysates (colon cancer cell-HCT, prostate cancer cell line PC-3, and human breast adenocarcinoma cell line-MCF7). Step potential=0.005, Amplitude time=0.02s, Frequency=10Hz, Scan Rate=0.05V/s. B) DPVs of Au/GQDs/P-Cys/AuNP-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA/p53 in 0.1MPBS+0.03mM thionine+1mM H2O2 in the presence of normal lysates (L929), and cancer cell lysates (colon cancer cell-HCT, prostate cancer cell line PC-3, and human breast adenocarcinoma cell line-MCF7). Step potential=0.005V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation amplitude=0.025V (Ag/AgCl), Modulation time=0.05s, Interval time=0.1s, Scan Rate=0.01V/s.
Comparison of different electrochemical methods for the detection of p53.

row.entry: Cell lysates. Normal and cancerous human skin fibroblast cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The electrochemical peak height of tumor suppressor protein p53 detection in normal and various cancer cells lysates using Au/P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs-streptavidin-HRP-BSA-Ab immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensing of tumor suppressor protein p53 in unprocessed human plasma and cell lysates using a novel nanocomposite based on poly-cysteine/graphene quantum dots/gold nanoparticle

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Higher Education Institute of Rab-Rashid, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Research Center, Uremia University, Uremia, 57154, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nano Technology, Faculty of Science, Uremia University, Uremia, 57154, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensor for quantitation of tumor suppressor protein p53 based on ternary signal amplification strategy was fabricated. In this work, p53-antibody was immobilized onto a green and biocompatible nanocomposite containing poly l-cysteine (P-Cys) as conductive matrix and graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/gold nanoparticles (GNPs) as dual amplification elements. Therefore, a novel multilayer film based on P-Cys, GQDs, and GNPs was exploited to develop a highly sensitive immunosensor for detection of p53. Fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer on a gold surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high amount of the anti-p53. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for p53 concentration was linear up to 0.000197-0.016 pM (by SWV technique) and 0.195–50 pM (by DPV technique) with lower limit of quantification of 0.065 fM. Also, linear range and lower limit of quantification of p53 in unprocessed human plasma were 0.000592-1.296 pM and 0.065 fM, respectively. The method was applied to the assay of p53 in human plasma sample and normal and malignant cell line lysates such as normal cell Line from mouse C3H (L929), colon cancer cell-HCT, prostate cancer cell line PC-3, and human breast adenocarcinoma cell line-MCF7.

ce:section.ce:para: Early stage diagnosis of cancer by monitoring the cancer biomarkers in human biological fluids is the excellent tool and the first step toward adopting efficient therapy, hindering metastasis and reducing mortality rate. p53 plays a critical role in apoptosis [1]. Defective p53 could allow irregular cells to proliferate and lead to cancer [2]. 50% of all human tumors contain p53 mutants. In normal cells, the p53 protein level is low. But after damage of p53 gene, tumor suppression is strictly reduced. From the analytical point of view, it should be noted that, the typical mean value of p53 protein in the serum is 150ng/L [1,2]. The level of p53 in human serum can be reasonably accurate in reflecting tissue alterations in p53 at the gene and/or protein level, thus leading to a potential convenient and noninvasive tumor screening approach.

On such a basis, much attention has been devoted to developing procedures to detect the presence of p53 protein at very low concentrations in a physiological environment and biological fluids. Among these procedures, the most common ones are traditional colorimetric methods, such as the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [3] and [4], which require multiple steps, and rather long incubation periods. In order to meet the increasing demand for early and ultrasensitive detection of diagnostic biomarker of p53 proteins, various signal amplification technologies using nanomaterials have been developed in the past decade: (i) one of the most popular strategies is nanoparticles used as scaffolds to load a large amount of antibodies to enhance the detection sensitivity [5]. (ii) Enzyme functionalized nanoparticles used as tracers to amplify the detection signal by loading a large number of enzymes towards an individual sandwich immunological reaction event [6]. (iii) Nanoparticles are used as carriers to fill a great number of electroactive species to amplify the electrochemical responses [7]. These nanomaterials include Au nanoparticles [8], graphene oxide [8], graphene [9], Au nanorods [6], carbon nanospheres [7] and carboxylated magnetic beads [7]. As the interaction of antibody with antigen is insufficient to produce a sensitive electrochemical test signal directly, great progress is alternative by using advanced nanomaterial as amplification elements [10].

Recently, GQDs have been explored for their fascinating electrical and optical features and employed in variety of applications, such as photovoltaics, bioimaging, fuel cells and biosensing purposes. GQDs have distinct advantages over semiconductor inorganic QDs, being abundant due to their carbon base, lower toxicity, high solubility in various solvents and capability of being functionalized by attaching groups at their edges. In addition, due to their semi-molecular characteristics, they can be easily handled in contrast with colloidal semiconductor QDs [11]. Interestingly, GQDs having the nano-scaled graphene structure, exhibit both the properties of nanomaterials and desirable physical features of the other graphene-based structures, such as carboxylic acid functional groups and the π-π conjugated network [12,13]. The GQDs are usually in the size range of 3–20nm and have proven multivalent redox behavior in different sizes of 2.2±0.3, 2.6±0.2, and 3.0±0.3, which can be recorded by voltammetry techniques and utilized for electrochemical sensing approaches [14]. Besides numerous reports of optical biosensors based on GQDs, electrochemical GQDs-modified biosensors have been recently reported, as it has been demonstrated that GQDs possess superior electron donor and acceptor capabilities compared to microsized graphene sheet along with greater number of carboxyl groups and smaller dimensions compared to other carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and carbon nanotubes [15,16]. Anisotropic gold nanostructures exhibit varying optical and electrocatalytical behaviors as a function of their shape and size. Up to now, several studies toward synthesis of shape-controlled nanostructures via electrochemical deposition techniques have been conducted and their excellent electrocatalytic features for sensing analytes have been concluded. The relation between size and morphology of various gold nanostructures, i.e. roughness, dominant metal facet and surface area toward reduction of oxygen and oxidation of methanol [17–19] have been evaluated, which suggests the possibility of imparting higher sensitivity to electrochemical biosensors by tailoring the morphology of nanostructures. Additionally, gold nanostructure-deposited electrode surfaces provide huge surface area with numerous sites for biomolecule immobilization.

Fabrication of nanoparticles (NPs) into polymer structure is a new method to enhance conductivity of polymer matrix and is an excellent pathway for preparation of polymers with high electrical properties which improved by incorporation of NPs. Lately, application of poly-amino acids modified by NPs and the use of these nanocomposites for bioanalytical aims has been described in the literature [20]. One of important amino acids for this purpose is l-Cysteine (l-cys). According to sulfhydryl functional of l-cys, it could be excellent candidate for electropolymerization and lead to preparation of a conductive interface with entitled “poly-cysteine”. On the other hand, this polymeric matrix could be functionalized by NPs for better electrochemical performance.

In this work, considering benefits of the GQDs, GNPs, and poly-cysteine (P-Cys), we used these materials for the construction of a novel immunosensor to apply the synergy contributions on the enhancement of immunosensor characteristics. Biotinylated antibody of anti-p53 immobilized on the surface of prepared transducer (P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs) has been used for detection of p53 using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) techniques. In summary, we proposed an innovative electrochemical immunosensor for quantitative detection of p53 based on ternary signal amplification using P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs as transducing materials. Combining the sensitive signal enhancement ability of GQDs-GNPs and the stability of P-Cys, the resulting immunosensor displayed excellent electrochemical behavior for determination of p53 antigen. According to electrochemical properties and suitable electrical conductivity, P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs can be enhanced the intensity of electrochemical signals. So, the electron transfer efficiency could be improved. On the other hand, immobilizing amount of anti-p53 could be enhanced. Therefore, sensitivity of the sensor was improved using the proposed modifier. The proposed immunosensor showed excellent sensitivity as well as long-term stability.

l-cysteine, Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Tetrachloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was from Merck. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (0.1M) was prepared by Na2HPO4 (0.2M) and NaH2PO4 (0.2M) with 0.15M of NaCl in deionized water. Anti-human tumor suppressor protein p53 (anti-p53 antibody) and p53 were purchased as Eliza kit (Termo Fisher). Human blood samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran).

Human breast cancer cell line MCF-7, HCT, PC3, and mouse normal control cell line L929 were obtained from the Iranian National Cell Bank (Pasteur Institute, Tehran, Iran). Cells were maintained in a humidified water-jacked incubator with 5% CO2 at 37°C in RPMI-1640 supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum(FBS), penicillin (100U/mL), and streptomycin (100μg/mL) (all purchased from Sigma, Germany).

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm), containing Ag/AgCl-saturated KCl as reference, a platinum wire as counter electrodes and gold (Au) working electrode (d=2mm) which powered by an electrochemical system comprising of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT 302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands). The system was run on a PC using NOVA 1.11 software.

The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3kV, and chemical compositions of the GNP were analysed by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with the FE-SEM equipment.

Prior to the preparation of the GNPs/GQDs/P-Cys/Au electrode, the bare gold electrode was polished with aluminium polishing cloth for about ten minutes. Then the electrode was immersed in 1:1 water/acetone mixture and rinsed with deionized water to eliminate organic contaminants. The last step of the cleaning was performed by electro-polishing the electrode in 0.5M H2SO4 solution by performing cyclic voltammetry in the range of −0.3 to 0.8V with 15 consecutive cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electropolymerization of l-cys on the surface of Au
ce:section.ce:para: There have been several reports for the fabrication of biosensors based on electrodeposition of amino acids such as l-arginine, l-proline, l-Aspartic acid, and l-cys on the surface of glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with applications in determination of drugs [21,22] and other chemicals [23–25]. The electrochemical polymerization of l-cys on Au electrode has been rarely reported [26]. Typically, the l-cys modification of Au electrode is carried out by dipping the electrode in a specific solution of cysteine, allowing for spontaneous assembly of l-cys molecules on the gold surface [27,28].

l-cys is a prominent amino acid, famous for carrying −thiol functional group in its side chain. It is known that cysteine anchors on gold surface, via establishing strong covalent bonding. Several approaches have been carried out to consider the characteristics of the Au-S bond. As it is evident in FTIR spectra of self-assembled layers of l-cys on Au electrode surface, the strong 2550cm−1 attributed to −SH functional group of l-cys disappeared after incubating Au electrode surface with the l-cys solution, proving the dissociation of S-H and chemisorption of l-cys on the gold substrate through Au-S bonding [29]. Recently, researchers [30] evaluated several factors affecting the strength of Au-S covalent bound either in self-assembled monolayer or in isolated single molecule mode by AFM-based SMFS. For example according to their study, at higher pH and increased incubation time, covalent contacts overweighs coordination bonds in single isolated molecules, while the surface properties, such as cleanness and incubation time affects the monolayer formation. The oxidized gold surface via electrochemical oxidation or chemical oxidation provides more strong Au-S covalent bonds in both cases.

Aiming to develop a uniform thin film of P-Cys on the surface of Au electrode, the electropolymerization of l-cys was implemented on the clean electrode by immersing electrode in 0.005M l-cys/PBS solution and applying CV in the potential range of −1 to 1V vs. Ag/AgCl for 10 cycles (Fig. 1 A). The Cysteine-coated Au electrode was rinsed with deionized water and prepared for the next modification step. Scheme 1 shows the possible mechanism for electropolymerization of l-cys on the surface of Au electrode which was confirmed by previous studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of GQDs on the surface of P-Cys modified Au electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene-based materials, such as graphene oxide, reduced-graphene oxide and graphene sheets, etc., have been widely incorporated in electrochemical biosensors for designing various sensing schemes. These materials have proved capability of accepting different roles (electrode surface modification, signal tag, etc.) owing to their unique mechanical properties, excellent electrical conductivity and catalytic behaviors [31–36]. GQD (synthesized according to our previous work [16]), the new member of the graphene-based materials, combines all the unique features of these materials with nano features, which makes it highly preferential for modifying electrochemical sensing platform. It is predicted that the free amine groups of the previously coated P-Cys layer, interacts with the oxygen bearing functional groups of GQDs, establishing highly powerful chemical bonding via amidation process, which helps stabilizing the electrodeposited GQDs layer [28].

For this purpose, the P-Cys-modified gold electrode was placed in the cell containing GQDs and subsequently, electrochemical deposition of GQDs on the electrode surface was conducted through CV with 30 consecutive cycles in the potential range of 0–1V vs. Ag/AgCl (Fig. 1B). l-cys has one NH2 groups and that can easily be oxidized to produce NH Covalent bond (CN) is then formed by the free radical reaction of NH … CO group of GQDs with excellent stability. Therefore, the covalent bond (OCNH) might be formed between carbonyl group of GQDs and the amino group of P-Cys. It is important to point out that during the high potential scan rate, the amino free radical will easily form and the thin layer of GQDs can be coated on the surface of P-Cys. Therefore, P-Cys-GQDs was prepared in this step. It is known that the electron withdrawing oxygen-containing groups on P-Cys are beneficial to the combination with carbon based materials from the electrostatic point of view. Therefore, the GQDs can be interacted with P-Cys via −NH3 + side groups, which allow forming distinctive self-assembly network between the GQDs. Finally, thin film of P-Cys-GQDs was obtained at the surface of Au electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sonoelectrodeposition of GNPs on the surface of P-Cys modified Au electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have revealed dependency of deposition potential on the size and morphology of generated GNPs. Shi et al. [37] demonstrated that the crystal density of generated GNPs increases by shifting toward negative electrodeposition potentials from 0.3 to −0.3 V(Ag/AgCl) on ITO electrode, because of increase in nucleation rate when oversaturation occurs at higher overpotentials. The reduction of AuCl4 − on Au electrode initiates at 0.15V(Ag/AgCl), leading to full nucleation at E=0.20V (Ag/AgCl), therefore, the potential was adjusted on E=0V (Ag/AgCl) where no reaction occurs [38]. For electrodeposition of gold nanoseeds on the surface of Au electrode pre-modified by P-Cys, the electrodeposition of metallic gold ions by chronoamperometry technique was carried out in the cell containing solution of 5mM HAuCl4 +0.5mM KCl. For preparation of GNPs, the P-Cys modified Au electrode was placed in the synthesis solutions comprising 5mM HAuCl4 +0.5mM KCl and sonoelectrodeposition was performed at −1.8V (Ag/AgCl) for 500s, while the synthesis solution and also the Au electrode surface were irradiated by ultrasound wave of 35W power (Fig. 1C). Therefore, AuCl4 − ions was electrostatically absorbed on the surface of P-Cys-GQDs and cause to preferential growth of Au by the diffusion-limited aggregation mechanism.

For preparation of immunosensor, Au pre-modified electrode (Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs) was incubated with Streptavidin-HRP for 30min in 37°C. The electrode (Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/Streptavidin-HRP) was washed by wash buffer (PBS with 1% tween 20). For minimizing the non-specific binding, Au/P-Cys-GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin-HRP electrode was incubated in BSA solution (5%) for 10min in 37°C. Using this step, free sites of electrode was blocked. Then, the electrode was cleaned by wash buffer (PBS with 1% tween). At the next step, 10μL of biotinylated anti-p53 was casted on the surface of Au/P-Cys-GNPs-streptavidin-HRP electrode and incubated in 4°C for 10h. The electrodes were thoroughly rinsed in 0.1% Tween-PBS (pH 8.5) to remove the unbound anti-p53. For minimizing the non-specific binding, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab was incubated in BSA solution (5%) for 2h. Using this step, free sites of GNPs was blocked. Then, the electrode was cleaned by wash buffer (PBS with 1% tween). The immunosensors were then directly subjected to immunochemical reaction with p53 protein and to electrochemical analysis. For p53 sensing, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA was incubated with different concentrations of p53 for 10min. Prior to electrochemical measurements, the immunosensors were washed thoroughly with 0.1% Tween- PBS (pH 7.4). Scheme 2 summarizes the fabrication steps of the immunosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM and EDX characterization of the modified sensing interface
ce:section.ce:para: SEM imaging was applied to evaluate the morphology of the electrodeposited layers. The SEM images of the Au electrode surfaces related to GNPs, P-Cys, P-Cys/GNPs, P-Cys/GQDs, P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs, and P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/Streptavidin-HRP were represented in (Fig. 2 A–F). The SEM images reveal scattered particles of l-cys in broad size range with relatively low coverage (Fig. 2B), the evenly deposited, and closely packed GQDs layer in approximate size range of 30nm (Fig. 2D) and the uniform GNPs layer with particles of similar non-spherical morphology related to Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs (Fig. 2A,C,E). Interestingly, after incubation of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs with streptavidin-HRP, the morphology of electrode was changed which confirmed binding of GNPs with streptavidin-HRP. Importantly, the SEM image of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs image shows a number of aggregated bright dots positioned over the entire surface particularly at the wrinkle sites, revealing that the GNPs were indeed self-assembled onto the surface of Au/P-Cys/GQDs. Interestingly, more dispersed and less aggregated GNPs are seen upon the electrostatic interactions of Streptavidin-HRP with the GNP surface (Fig. 2F).

The EDX characterization technique was employed to further study the formation Au/P-Cys, Au/P-Cys/GNPs, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs, and Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/Streptavidin-HRP (Fig. 3 A–D). As evident in Fig. 3(A–C), signals for C, N, S and O elements belong to both cysteine and GQDs layers, while N, S is only attributed to P-Cys layer, as cysteine include amine and SH groups. The large and sharp Au peak is the typical signal for bulk gold element. The corresponding EDX spectra of P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs demonstrates clearly indicates the electrodeposition of nano-size gold layer on the surface of P-Cys-GQDs was perfumed successfully. Also, after immobilization of Streptavidin-HRP on P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs, some of new peaks was appeared which related to iron and phosphor and confirmed the binding of Streptavidin-HRP with P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs (Fig. 3D).

Different types of modified electrodes (Bare Au, Au/P-Cys, Au/P-Cys/GQDs, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-Streptavidin-HRP, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA, and Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-Streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/BSA/p53) were evaluated for their electrochemical behavior in the solution containing 2mL PBS. As shown in Fig. 4 (A and B), no peak was observed using Au electrode in the applied potential range of 0–0.5V (Ag/AgCl) by SWV technique. But, after modification of Au electrode by P-Cys a new peak was observed about −0.25V vs. Ag/AgCl. Interestingly, electrodeposition of GQDs on the surface of Au electrode modified by P-Cys, lead to little increasing the peak current due to due to increase in the surface area and conductivity of GQDs. Importantly, after sonooelectrodeposion of GNPs on the surface of Au/P-Cys/GQDs, the peak current was increased markedly. The reason of the peak current increasing after this step is acceleration electron transfer effect of GNPs. The decrease of the overvoltage of Au/P-Cys/GQDs after deposition of GNPS obviously indicates the crucial role of Au/P-Cy/GQDs/GNPs for signal amplifications. This approach confirmed the successful electrodeposition of gold nanostructures on the surface of Au/P-Cys. It has been previously reported that upon immobilizing antibodies onto the electrode surface, the net charge of the antibody will determine the surface charge of the electrode and when antigen molecules are present in the solution, the recognition immunoreaction will occur, which can alter the surface charge of the electrode [39]. However, the immobilization of the streptavidin-HRP on the surface of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs led to a decrease in the peak current because of hindered electron transfer effect of streptavidin. For minimizing the non-specific binding, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/streptavidin-HRP was incubated in BSA solution (5%) for 10min in 37°C. Using this step, free sites of electrode was blocked. Then, the electrode was cleaned by wash buffer (PBS with 1% tween). At the next step, 10μL of biotinylated anti-p53 was casted on the surface of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/streptavidin-HRP/BSA electrode and incubated in 4°C for 10h. The electrodes were thoroughly rinsed in 0.1% Tween-PBS (pH 8.5) to remove the unbound anti-p53. It’s observed that, a decline in peak current was observed for a second time after immobilization of biotinilated-anti-p53, which indicates that anti-p53 has been successfully immobilized on the surface of P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/streptavidin-HRP/BSA. After blocking the nonspecific binding sites by BSA, the peak current increased. This could be attributed to the blocking effect of BSA and activation of specific binding sites. Following immersing the prepared electrode in solution of p53 protein, the peak current was decreased again, because of the creation of the immunocomplex blocking layer. The immunosensors were then directly subjected to immunochemical reaction with p53 protein and to electrochemical analysis. For p53 sensing, P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab was incubated with different concentrations of p53 for 10min. Prior to electrochemical measurements, the immunosensors were washed thoroughly with 0.1% Tween-PBS (pH 7.4). All of this procedure, was further confirmed by DPV technique. The comparison of DPVs and SWVs in the absence and presence of p53 tumor supressor protein using P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab demonstrated interaction of antibody-antigen was happen in this stage. The next considerable change upon recognition of antigen by the prepared immunosensor is evident in Fig. 4, where oxidation peak potential was decreased and confirmed successful recognition of p53 tumor suppressor protein.

Finally, these results confirmed that prepared nanocomposite could accelerate the rate of electron transfer which is necessary for redox reaction. Therefore, −Cys/GQDs/GNPs is a suitable mediator to shuttle electron between p53 and working electrode. This observation is also linked to the high conductivity and inherent ability of P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs. In addition, that might be related to the excellent properties of P-Cys as conductive matrix and GQDs plus GNPs as signal amplification elements.

Electrochemical characterization of the prepared immunosensor was investigated by DPV and SWV techniques. The voltammograms obtained by Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/p53 in 0.1M PBS (pH=7.4), 0.1M PBS+0.067M thionine, and 0.1M PBS+0.03M thionine, 0.001M H2O2 have been demonstrated in Fig. 5 (A-E). As can be seen in Fig. 5(A and B), Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs/streptavidin-HRP/BSA/Ab/p53 shows a little peak in about −0.2V (Ag/AgCl) which related to the electroactivity of the prepared electrode.

After addition of thionine, the oxidation peak of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab/p53 was increased which were measured with DPV, SWV techniques and appeared at about −0.18V, −0.16V, respectively. A significant increase was observed in the intensity of the redox peaks of thionine immediately after addition of H2O2. After injection of H2O2 to the electrochemical cell, the voltammogram just shifted to negative potentials without any redox peaks (because of the absence of any electroactive media). However, after injection of thionine to the electrochemical cell, the redox peaks of thionine was immediately appeared with the collected height of self-redox peaks of thionine along with electrocatalytical activity of HRP on the redox peaks of thionine in the presence of H2O2 as activating agent of HRP enzyme. This current amplification was due to electron transfer facilitation of thionine in the presence of H2O2. There was also a little negative shift in potential of all redox peaks of thionine. This significant incensement approves that the preparation of immunosensor have been done successfully and more oxidized thionine is produced due to the activity of HRP molecule on Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab/p53. Therefore, thionine is able to performance as an excellent mediator to shuttle from electrons from the redox center of HRP to the surface of electrode. These results are consistent with those reported by others [40]. According to previous studies, the electron transfer between the enzyme and electrode surface is facilitated using a mediator (thionine in this study).Therefore, these results verify that HRP is successfully linked to the electrode surface and retains its high catalytic activity. The results also provide strong evidence that using of thionine as a perfect electron transfer mediator can amplify the electrochemical response of the Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab for detection of p53 tumor suppressor protein.

The standard p53 samples in assay buffer (PBS with 1% Tween 20,10 BSA), were used to evaluate the sensitivity of the prepared p53immunosensor by employing SWV and DPV techniques. In order to develop a voltammetric method for determining of p53, we selected the DPV and SWV modes, because the peaks are sharper and better defined at lower concentration of p53 than those obtained by CV, with a lower background current, resulting in improved resolution. According to the obtained results, it was possible to apply this technique to the quantitative analysis of p53. The PBS of pH 7.40 was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of p53 as it gave maximum peak current at physiological pH. The peak at about −0.20V (Ag/AgCl) was considered for the analysis SWVs/DPVs obtained with increasing amounts of P53 showed that the peak current increased linearly with increasing concentration, as shown in Fig. 6 A and B.

Using the optimum conditions described above, SWVs and DPVs of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab were recorded and calibration curves were obtained (Inset of Fig. 6A and B). The analytical results were summarized in Table 1 . The results exhibited that this method performed with a higher sensitivity when it was compared to other conventional detection approaches [41–45]. Table 1 compares the main features of the developed immunosensor with other electrochemical immunosensors described so far for the determination of both phosphorylated and non-phosphorylated human p53. Although the linear range achieved is not as low as those reported by other authors, all of them using nanomaterials-based amplification systems, this immunosensor offered a sensitivity adequate for the determination of the target protein in unprocessed biological fluid (plasma sample). It is worth remarking, also, the inherent simplicity of the method reported here can be also claimed as an important practical advantage versus all of other approaches reported so far, which require multiple reagents and complex and time-consuming substrate/nanomaterials modification protocols, thus making them difficult to be considered as suitable tools for the development of user-friendly devices for on-site determination of this relevant biomarker.

It can be seen that the Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab offered good LLOQ for p53 detection and the detection limit was lower than some of previous reports. These results indicated that Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab is an appropriate platform for the determination of p53 in biological fluids. On the other word, the prepared electrode shows voltammetric responses with low detection limit and suitable linear range for P53 in optimal conditions, which makes it suitable for determination of this antigen. It is found that, by incorporating P-Cys as conductive matrix and GQDs/GNP as a dual signal amplification elements, a novel strategy for developing an efficient and robust electrochemical sensing platform was established. The electrochemical sensor showed high sensitivity and simplicity for detection of p53.

Another advantage of the proposed immunosensor was that the resulting modified electrode showed good long term stability. Stability of the proposed electrode was tested by measuring the decrease in voltammetric current during repetitive DPV measurements of p53 with Au/P-Cys-GQDs-GNPs-streptavidin-HRP-BSA-Ab stored in −4°C. Obtained results show that this electrode has remarkable stability without significant change in the voltammetric currents. When the electrode was stored in the −4°C, the current response remained almost unchanged for about 8days. RSD of repeated peak currents was (5.9%). The high stability of the Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab could be related to the strong affinity of P-Cys for GQDs and GQDs for GNPs and the low solubility of the Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab in physiological pH of PBS. To check the inter-electrode reproducibility of the modified electrode, four modified electrodes were tested simultaneously by recording SWVs in buffer solution, containing 10ng/mL of p53. The relative standard deviation was 5.11%. Therefore, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab imparts a suitable stability onto measurements of p53 by SWV and DPV techniques.

According to the obtained results, it was possible to apply this technique to the quantitative analysis of p53 in real samples using DPV technique (Fig. 7 A and B). As mentioned previously, the PBS of pH 7.4 was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of p53. For this purpose, 0.5mL of unprocessed human plasma were taken and added into 0.1M PBS+0.03M thionine+0.001 M H2O2 to reach a total volume of 3mL.

Using DPV technique, the resulting sample solutions were measured using the prepared immunosensor. The corresponding analytical results displayed in Table 1. Excellent recoveries varying from 98.3% to102.1% were obtained, which illustrates that the established method are quite desirable and qualified for practical sample testing.

In addition, DPV techniques were used for the analysis of p53 in different cancer cell culture media. To this end, using the developed biosensor and standard addition method, we analysed for the detection of the p53 in normal cell (L929) and various cancer cell lysates such as breast cancer cell-MCF7, colon cancer cell-HCT, and prostate cancer cell line PC-3. The engineered immunosensor (Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab) could detect the p53 in these samples. Fig. 8 and Table 2 shows the SWVs and DPVs of p53 in the supernatants of the normal and various cancer cell lines cultured during three days. The application of the engineered immunosensor showed the highest peak current in PC3. Importantly, MCF-7 showed the lowest peak current than normal cell (L929) using SWVs. Also, the results show that the peak currents of HCT is near to L929. These results confirmed application of Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab for diagnosis of p53 in different cancer types and relationship of p53 with the investigated cancer cells. Therefore, Au/P-Cys/GQDs/GNPs-streptavidin/HRP/BSA/Ab can be used as a promising tool for diagnosis of cancer development and monitoring of patient therapy using p53 measurement.

We have developed an ultrasensitive electrochemical immunosensing for p53 detection based on P-Cys as conductive polymeric matrix and GQDs/GNPs as a synergetic signal amplification elements. For the first time, fully electrochemical methodology was used to prepare a new transducer for ternary signal amplification on a gold surface which provided a high surface area to immobilize a high surface concentration of the anti-p53. Also, GNPs were electrodeposited on the surface of P-Cys-GQDs with a new ultrasonic-assisted electrochemical synthesis method. Therefore, a green electrodeposition method was developed for the preparation of a new immunosensor. Experimental results show that, proposed immunoassay can effectively improve electron transfer efficiency based on synergistic effect of P-Cys as conductive matrix and GQDs/GNPs as dual amplification elements. It was found that, the new morphology of GNPs synthesized in the presence of soft template facilitated the electron transfer. Also, P-Cys and GQDs provided a biocompatible environment with a large specific surface area for the further immobilization of anti-p53. The immunosensor was employed for the detection of p53 in physiological pH. Under optimized condition the calibration curve for p53 concentration was 0.000197-0.016 pM (By SWV technique) and 0.00533-11.66 pM (By DPV technique) with low limit of quantification 0.065 fM. Also, linear range and lower limit of quantification of p53 in unprocessed human plasma were 0.000592-1.296 pM and 0.065 fM, respectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.
